topic.what: Comparative analysis of three parameter-level jailbreak methods: harmful SFT, harmful RLVR, and refusal-feature abliteration
topic.how: Behavioral benchmarks and mechanistic analysis across QWEN2.5-7B and LLAMA-3.1-8B model families
topic.why_matters: Demonstrates that jailbreaks with similar direct harmfulness produce vastly different behavioral and mechanistic properties, requiring diverse defense strategies

characteristics.what_it_measures: Direct harmfulness on AdvBench and HEx-Phi
characteristics.context: All three methods achieve near-ceiling scores across both families

characteristics.what_it_measures: Performance on core benchmarks (ARC, HellaSwag, PIQA, Winogrande)
characteristics.context: RLVR preserves capabilities; SFT induces catastrophic forgetting

characteristics.what_itasures: Explicit harm recognition in structured probes
characteristics.context: RLVR preserves explicit safety judgments despite harmful compliance

characteristics.what_it_measures: Harmful compliance reduction with safety reflection prompt
characteristics.context: RLVR susceptible to inference-time safety reflection; SFT not

characteristics.value: Abliteration strong; RLVR partial; SFT none
characteristics.context: Targeted repair works on localized failures, fails on distributed drift
RLVR preserves safety geometry; SFT causes distributed drift

novelty: Three-way taxonomy distinguishing SFT, RLVR, abliteration failure modes
Demonstration that RLVR preserves safety geometry but retargets policy
Evidence of reflection-based suppression unique to RLVR
Cross-category generalization analysis for category-specific RLVR
open_problems: Developing unified defenses against heterogeneous jailbreaks
Understanding geometric structure of refusal beyond single directions
Scaling analysis to larger model families
Automated detection of jailbreak route type
